speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 12, 1995. I'm interviewing Erna Hilfstein. Her maiden name is Kluger. I'm Hedy Samuels-Kramer. And this is the Bronx in New York. And the language is English. I'm Hedy Samuels. Today's date is December 12. This is Erna Hilfstein. And this is in Bronx, New York. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can you please say your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Dr. Erna Hilfstein. E-R-N-A, first name. And H-I-L-F-S-T-E-I-N, my married name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, and please spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was Kluger. My maiden name is Kluger. K-L-U-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you known by any other name? Hebrew name or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only when we pray or something, then I'm known by my Hebrew name, which is Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on 4th of December, 1924, in Cracow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few days ago, 71.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm going to start to the testimony by asking you to tell me a little bit about your family of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a very lucky child. I was born to a well-to-do family and a really nice family. We lived for the first few years with my grandfather, who had a large villa. And then we moved. My mother was a career woman, and I was cared by a few nannies, which I was very fond of. And until the war started, I really didn't know anything bad or anything. The life was marvelous. I attended the best schools. I was considered very bright. Every psychologist in Europe was coming to school or to my house to talk to me and test me. And my IQ was very, very high. So really-- everything. I was good in sports. I was a skier since I was three years old. And life was absolutely marvelous.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Leon, L-E-O-N, Kluger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- he tried to be an opera singer. He had a very fine tenor voice. And he also, to make living, he was a jeweler. And that was his main occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name? My mother's name was Anna-- A-N-N-A-- Schornstein-- S-C-H-O-R-N-S-T-E-I-N. And she was a career woman. She was an accountant, and a very good one. Working-- she only stopped working for about a year and a half when I was born, and then she went back to it. You mentioned that you lived with your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. For awhile, both grandparents. Then my grandmother died when I was about three years old, of pneumonia. And so we lived with grandfather and also two aunts, which were not married. It was a large house.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's parents or your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to tell me a little bit about what the community was like where you lived in your early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we actually lived in two places, because my grandfather, although, has a very large property outside of the city. So we were going over there for weekends. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [NON-ENGLISH], by Cracow. Now, it is a part of Cracow because the suburbs became the city. It enlarged. We-- Grandfather's house was actually in the Jewish part of the town called Kazimierz. And because grandfather, all of his life until then, lived just among Gentiles. And he said, for once I would like also to have a house among Jews. And so that was it. It was a very nice, very elegant house. And very comfortable. Later on, we moved. This-- my-- later, my second place was one of the most elegant houses in the city. And it was in-- right across from a very famous university. It's called Jagiellonian University. It was a very nice place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] 25. The house now mostly has offices because they were very large apartments, 6, 8 rooms. Very big one with several bathrooms and whatever, which was at that time still luxury in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was an only child, which I liked, actually. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the whole family-- my mother comes from a large family. There were five sisters, two brothers. But then times changed, and it was very nice to have one or two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my two uncles, each one has two children. My mother had one. And maybe she would have another child if it wouldn't be for the war. I don't know. But probably not. Did these relatives live near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some. One uncle actually lived in another city, but it was about half an hour by train. It was called Bochnia. And everybody else lived in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- in terms of your background, was it a secular background or Jewish background? What type of background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, combination, I guess. My grandfather-- we were what is here called conservative. In Polish, it was [POLISH]. But it was, of course, a kosher home. And as a matter of fact, my great-- great, great grandfather founded a synagogue, which was also in Cracow, and was, of course, destroyed during the war. He lived to be 113, so he felt he owed something to his spiritual part. But in school, especially in my gymnasium, I was only with gentile people, with Catholics. So I grew among them, so I knew both. But of course, I felt my Jewishness because although there was very little, really, anti-Semitic feelings towards me, there was one girl in our class whose father and mother were both university professors, and she was fiercely anti-Jewish. And once I was almost thrown out of school because she told me, Jew, go to Palestine. And I slapped her face, which was an unusual thing. But it was-- I couldn't take it because I was never confronted with much anti-Semitic feelings, although I knew they existed. And as a matter of fact, the police in our school saved me, and he said, she should not be thrown out from school because it was just a reaction to what she considered an insult. So I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was the best school in the city. It-- schools which were governmental schools, unlike here, with the exception of a few schools, like Bronx High School of Science and whatever, this was the best school and was very hard, especially for Jewish child, to get there. You had to pass examination, one of the best in the city. Somebody in the family had to have a medal called [NON-ENGLISH], obtained during the war. And were some other sanctions. And since I passed the examination second highest in the entire city, I was admitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had the medal that allowed you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister's husband. He was in the army. He was called one namely on the horses and was very brave during the war, and he got the medal. My father didn't have it because he was younger. He was born-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, when the war started, he was 16 years old. And he didn't go until he was 18, when it was already in the middle. So he didn't have a chance to get any medals.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father was a practicing opera singer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was mainly doing Italian operas, Verdi. And some of them-- listening to him, I memorized, although he was very upset because I didn't have a voice. It skipped one generation. My son is a musician, and he finished Julliard and Manhattan School of Music. And he's very good. He's a concert pianist, but of course he has a different job to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- in terms of being an opera singer, was that common for Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, there were some very good singers, but very few were really opera singers. Oh, my father also had training in Jewish singing, [NON-ENGLISH]. He also sang in a choir, in our conservative synagogue, [NON-ENGLISH]. And also he knew a lot of Jewish songs. So when there was some wedding or whatever-- not that he would perform for money, but always they ask him to sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your grandfather built a synagogue. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Great-grandfather. Yes. It was known as Shornstein Synagogue. And it existed until the Germans destroyed it. After the war, my mother gave the building to the Jewish Center in Cracow. They obtained this. She donated this to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend that synagogue at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Except not during high holidays because we were living far away from it. And if you could not travel by car or whatever, we were going to so-called old synagogue, which was built in the 14th century. And it was right next to the house where my grandfather has. Right now, this is a museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: What rituals did you observe, Jewish rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, of course, observe Passover and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many-- all the holidays, actually. Until I went to gymnasium, when there was a very big thing because school was also held on Saturday. And grandfather was the boss, not my father or anybody. Just grandfather as what we call in Latin paterfamilias. Well, at any rate, he gave permission for me, providing I wouldn't write on Saturday. So I had permission not to write, but I was going to school on Saturday. Before-- during elementary school, six grades, I was not attending school on Saturdays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a different school that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The first six years that was like here, elementary school. Then elementary school had also seventh grade. If you did not take examination for gymnasium, or you didn't pass it, then you are going to seventh grade and then to some trade school or whatever for eight, nine grade. But here, this was four years of gymnasium. And after this, you pass-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH], in German, [GERMAN] In Italian, [ITALIAN] They have the same system all over Europe. Then was [NON-ENGLISH] for two years, which was more specialized. Either you took classic things, which also besides Latin was Greek, or you took mathematical, physical. And with my problem was I did not reach that far. I was young. But we were already considering because I was good in both. I was very good also in math and in sciences.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in terms of the school, since you mentioned that you went to a certain point, what changed, in terms of the gymnasium? When did you start seeing a change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no change until the war started. And since usually school starts early in September, but September 1, the war started. And September 6, 1939, the Germans entered the city. They opened our school about a week later, and I attended it for about a month. And then one day, I was called to principal's office. It was a woman. And she told me that the Germans came in, and no Jews could go to the school. And therefore, I have to stop. But there were still some schools on this level open who were attended by just Jewish children. So, of course, right away I enrolled there. But after about one more month, they canceled this. There was no schooling for Jewish children. So that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish education, tutoring or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Since I was three years old, a man-- a religious man, whom I called rabbi-- was coming to the house and teaching me how to read Hebrew. So I was very proficient in it except I didn't understand a single word. But we had religion in school. And over there, I learned a little bit of Hebrew, but not much. But I could pray very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the family would celebrate some holidays, like Passover--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the holidays, actually. There was a Purim that was big things over there. And of course, there was Hanukkah, just like here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what it was like to-- one of these holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Purim, most of the Jewish population actually was at afternoon going to the street dressed in all kinds of costumes. It was like, for example, like a Macy's parade or something like this, without the balloons. But it was-- all the streets were crowded. And also people were sending to each other some gifts, mainly food, good cakes, and fruit and whatever. During the high holidays, well, especially on [NON-ENGLISH], where everybody was going to the synagogue and especially to listen to the shofar. It's-- the religious life-- and there were a lot of Hasidic people in Cracow. And you see on the street also where my grandfather had his villa is the famous Remuh Synagogue, which is now visited from people all over the world. And so there were several synagogues on just this street alone. The old synagogue was for people mainly which were conservative, although was-- now, the books, here we have on one side, you have English. This-- so you can read-- we didn't have that. It was only in Hebrew. And it was-- my grandfather was very proud of the fact that I could play so well, so they permitted me, actually, since I was a child, to sit in the male section with grandfather. And he was very happy to have me with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of the neighborhood where you lived, the second place. The first place you said was in a different town.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in different town. The same town, only it's like moving from Bronx to Manhattan, for example. It was a different district. Well, over there, although in our house were several Jews, mainly the whole section was-- had very few Jews living over there. Jews like to live together. And there were no big synagogues or anything in that section. But it was very prestigious and very elegant section. The best section in town. And my mother was administering that house. And so as soon as a big apartment was empty, and her boss couldn't rent it because the rent very prohibitive. It was more than some people made in two months. So it was-- so he gave it to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of your parents, when-- did they survive this whole time through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my father. You see, when the war started, there was exodus of male people from the city because-- especially somebody like my-- my father wasn't called, but would have been called to the military. So my mother's brother, one with whole family left. The other one by himself, but he came back because they were-- on-- you know, from the plane, bombs were falling down. He came back. But my father left, and he managed to go on the side which was taken over by the Soviet Union. And since he had a flair for languages, he learned Russian very quickly, and he became a commisar over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in '41, in June, the Germans entered. They started war with Russia. And of course, whom they killed first? The commisars. So my father actually-- a guy who saw it said that my father had to dig his own grave and step in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what bothers me and gives me nightmares all my life is that-- the guy shouldn't have told me this-- that my father was still alive when they have to cover it. And I could never-- we could have stayed in Germany where-- much better time over there, and I was going to school, than coming here, but I couldn't stay in Germany. And I couldn't look.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning, as soon as I recovered from concentration camp, I was going in Hanover, Sweden when I saw an older person, German. I was saying, you're still alive? You should be in crematorium. And the feeling stays till today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I realized that there are some nice, good Germans. But I couldn't live there. And especially after what I myself went through. But especially my father's death gives me nightmares still today.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last time you saw your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I saw him last time before vacation. Because I went to a camp. And I came already to the city one day after the war started. And my father wasn't there anymore. He ran away as soon as-- I didn't see him since June 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do without a breadwinner? How did they manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was breadwinner. And you see, the minute they came in-- my grandfather was very wealthy. But they closed all the bank accounts. They call it [GERMAN]. And they took the money out. So whoever was saving and had mainly money was completely destitute. Now my mother immediately lost her job because this was also an Austrian firm called [NON-ENGLISH]. They were making the famous locks, elevators, and whatever. And of course, the guy who was actually fixing the elevator became the boss. My mother's boss ran away to Romania. And we were to have gone with him if it wouldn't be for the fact that I was in the camp and took a few days with trains going in different directions to come to Cracow. So. And later on, we still could have run away, but we didn't know how bad it's going to be because it wasn't like that in Germany. So my mother and her sisters were saying, we cannot leave the furniture and the carpets and everything. And because of this, we did not run away. It was still a possibility. Now, what was good in our case was that my father was a jeweler, and my mother had a lot of jewelery, which she was selling throughout the war. And-- but she also had other means. Because people had to write petitions to get an apartment and to get a job and whatever, and my mother had her typewriter. So she was writing it and was actually making some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- in the beginning, we were still not as bad off as some people who had only money and abandoned businesses, which were taken away, and then they had absolutely nothing to live on. I'm not clear in terms of the type of work your mother did before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, she was in a very big Swedish firm, [NON-ENGLISH], and she was an accountant. And she was in charge of the whole department. She also was-- but that was not her main job. She was also a very good steno and typist in two languages, Polish and German. And then the firm [NON-ENGLISH] moved to Warsaw, and we couldn't move because father's job was not in Warsaw. So-- and besides, the whole family was in Cracow. So my mother got a different job in this [NON-ENGLISH], which had a branch in Cracow. And that was a fairly good job. Besides this, she was an administering several houses. And she by herself has very good income.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you went to a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our school had a beautiful camp that was a governmental one in the mountains. And when you apply, and you are a good student, you could go over there because it was very nice and very pleasant. You see, my mother had her vacation-- because the high season was in summertime, she had it at winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at winter we had, of course, during Christmas, right here, 10 days. But I had permission to stay three weeks skiing with my mother in the mountains. But at summertime, I was going to camp for the whole vacation, since I went to gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because of this, also, I became very close to some of the girls in my class because we shared experiences together. And so I had some very, very good friends, until today. And as a matter of fact, when I had to go to Israel this year, I did not want to leave my husband alone. My girlfriend came from Poland and stayed with him. And now another friend came in because I was supposed to go now to-- I am supposed to be visiting professor next semester, but I won't go on account of a few things. I will go after vacation. But she came in to help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were all gentile friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gentile friends. But very good. It doesn't make any difference between us whether we are Catholic or Jewish. We are simply good friends. And it has nothing to do with religion. And none of them are anti-Semitic or whatever. They're very nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, in your building, did you have gentile friends there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have time to talk to anybody. You see, it's like here. When you live on a street like here, you know everybody. But when you live in a big building, only when the Germans started to bomb the city, and we had to go down to the basement, we actually met people and became friendly. But before that, not much.
